coredata.dc:title: Solvent-controlled synthesis of full-color carbon dots and its application as a fluorescent food-tasting sensor for specific recognition of jujube species
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Guannan
Kong, Dehao
Han, Jiayin
Zhou, Ri
Gao, Yuan
Wu, Zepei
Zhao, Lianjing
Wang, Chenguang
Wang, Lijun
Lu, Geyu
coredata.dc:description: Full-color luminescent carbon dots (CDs) possess enormous feasible applications in fluorescence sensing. However, the modulation of luminescent emission requires proper synthesis strategies with distinctive precursors to regulate the surface chemistry of the CDs. In this work, the full-color CDs were prepared with the same reactants by adjusting the reaction solvent to cover the entire spectral range. The factors that determined their luminescence caused by solvents were discussed. We found that CDs had a rapid sensing ability to Fe3+ with a wide detection range and high accuracy. Moreover, the discriminant model of jujube species depending on internal Fe3+ content was established successfully using principal component analysis and Bayesian discriminant analysis. This study provides a novel perspective for the preparation of full-color CDs by the same reactants and provides strong application potential in the identification of jujube species by a fluorescent sensor.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Full-color CDs
Formation mechanism
Fluorescent sensor
Species discriminant

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selectivity and quantitative analysis for Fe3+
Reaction of CDs with jujube juice extraction solution

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A solvent-engineered strategy for synthesis of full-color CDs using citric acid and melamine as starting materials.
TEM and HRTEM images (inset) of (A) B-CDs, (B) G-CDs, (C) Y-CDs, and (D) R-CDs. Particle size distribution diagrams of (E) B-CDs, (F) G-CDs, (G) Y-CDs, and (H) R-CDs.
FT-IR spectra of the four selected CDs.
High-resolution XPS C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s spectra of (A) B-CDs, (B) G-CDs, (C) Y-CDs, and D) R-CDs.
Fluorescence excitation−emission map and PL spectra of (A)(E)B-CDs, (B)(F)G-CDs, (C)(G) Y-CDs and (D)(H)R-CDs.
Schematic representation of possible formation mechanism and electronic structure for CDs with tunable PL emission.

(A) Comparison of PL intensities of CDs after adding different metal ions with the same concentration of 1.25 mM. (B-E) The relationship between the four selected CDs and the concentration of Fe3+ ions. Inset: Emission spectra recorded with different concentrations of Fe3+ ions respectively.
(A) Fluorescence spectra of R-CDs reacted with jujube samples; (B) Spatial distribution of R-CDs reacted with jujube samples based on PCA.
Comparison of the optical/fluorescence based methods on the detection of Fe3+.

head.ce:title: Solvent-controlled synthesis of full-color carbon dots and its application as a fluorescent food-tasting sensor for specific recognition of jujube species

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory on Integrated Optoelectronics, Key Laboratory of gas Sensors, College of Electronic Science and Engineering, Jilin University, 2699 Qianjin Street, Changchun, Jilin Province, 130012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory on Integrated Optoelectronics
College of Electronic Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: . State Key Laboratory on Integrated Optoelectronics, Key Laboratory of gas sensors, College of Electronic Science and Engineering, Jilin University, 2699 Qianjin Street, Changchun, Jilin Province, 130012, China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Luminescence and Applications, Changchun Institute of Optics, Fine Mechanics and Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun, 130033, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Luminescence and Applications

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: . State Key Laboratory of Luminescence and Applications, Changchun Institute of Optics, Fine Mechanics and Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun 130033, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Full-color luminescent carbon dots (CDs) possess enormous feasible applications in fluorescence sensing. However, the modulation of luminescent emission requires proper synthesis strategies with distinctive precursors to regulate the surface chemistry of the CDs. In this work, the full-color CDs were prepared with the same reactants by adjusting the reaction solvent to cover the entire spectral range. The factors that determined their luminescence caused by solvents were discussed. We found that CDs had a rapid sensing ability to Fe3+ with a wide detection range and high accuracy. Moreover, the discriminant model of jujube species depending on internal Fe3+ content was established successfully using principal component analysis and Bayesian discriminant analysis. This study provides a novel perspective for the preparation of full-color CDs by the same reactants and provides strong application potential in the identification of jujube species by a fluorescent sensor.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have been intensively studied owing to their attractive fluorescence properties [1–3]. CDs are a kind of carbon material with a size of less than 10 nm, which has the properties of wavelength-dependent photoluminescence (PL) related to size and excitation wavelength [4]. Since CDs were discovered by Xu in 2004 [4], many methods to prepare CDs have been extensively studied by researchers. So far, CDs have been successfully prepared by various methods, including arc-discharge, hydrothermal, microwave treatment, and electrochemical oxidation [5–8].

Although the fluorescent CDs have been studied for many years, the fluorescence mechanism and formation processes of CDs are still not definitely clear. Four effect theories have been successively developed and widely accepted, including quantum size effect, surface state effect, molecule state and carbon-core state effect, and crosslink enhanced emission (CEE) effect. Researchers claimed that the fluorescence could be tuned by adjusting particle size and conjugate π-domains of CDs [9–11]. Surface states are also considered as an essential factor in the fluorescence mechanism. Sun et al. from Clemson University demonstrated that surface defects or passivation will lead to surface energy traps and then control the CDs fluorescence [12]. Hetero atoms-doping strategy was proposed to modulate surface states and optical properties of CDs effectively [13]. Giannelis et al. considered that molecular states and carbon-core states play a decisive role in fluorescence mechanism [14]. Molecular fluorophores build up at low temperatures while carbon core starts build up at high temperatures. Dong et al. developed lipophilic red-emitting organic dots using the hydrogen-bond-induced variation of benzimidazole intermediate [15]. Yang and co-workers from Jilin University have put forward the crosslink enhanced emission (CEE) effect [16]. This mechanism believed that the crosslinked skeleton reduces the vibration and rotation of the fluorescence center in the CDs, causing the fluorescence intensity enhanced.

To date, the regulation of wide color gamut fluorescent CDs is a challenging task. Heretofore, a few papers have successfully reported the synthesis of fluorescent CDs covering the entire visible spectrum using a single precursor. For example, Xiong et al. reported a facile approach to preparing full-color CDs through column chromatography after hydrothermal reaction of urea and p-phenylenediamine [17]. Sun et al. synthesized multiple-color-emission CDs through thermal pyrolysis of citric acid and urea. By controlling the temperature and ratio of reactants, the emission peak red-shifted, covering the entire visible spectrum [18]. Jiang et al. obtained full-color emission tuning CDs via a solvothermal method of Phenylenediamines [19]. Although these works have successfully synthesized CDs with tunable PL, some challenges still remain. For example, CDs with tunable PL using solvent controlled synthesis of uniform precursors and reaction conditions. And there is controversy about the conclusions of the fluorescence mechanism and formation process [20,21].

Iron content plays an important role in controlling the stability of the living system and food efficacy [22]. Therefore, quantitative determination of Fe3+ is of great significance. A variety of chemical sensors have been developed for the detection of Fe3+, such as fluorescent metal nanoclusters [23], organic dyes [24], semiconductor quantum dots [25], fluorescent metal organic frameworks [26], etc. Compared to these optical/fluorescence based methods on the detection of Fe3+, CDs probes have the advantages of simple synthesis and outstanding selectivity. To the best of our knowledge, Fe3+ sensors for food grade determination have not been developed. Therefore, it is a great application prospect to establish a sensing platform for determining food grade based on CDs.

Herein, we report a novel one-pot synthesis to obtain fluorescent CDs with a wide color gamut covering the entire visible region. Citric acid and melamine were selected as reactants, and the PL emission region was controlled by regulating the type of the reaction solvent. Four different solvents were chosen to control the dehydration and carbonization process of CDs, which affected the quantum size, oxidation degree and concentration of graphitic nitrogen. Various characterizations were employed to figure out the core factors leading to the red-shift of CDs. Moreover, the comparison of metal ion detection capacity by full-color CDs was also discussed. The results showed that CDs can be used as a fluorescent sensor for Fe3+ ion detection with wide detection range and high accuracy. Finally, we designed a food-tasting sensor using principal component analysis and Bayesian discriminant analysis to distinguish and accurately predict jujube species according to their internal Fe3+ contents. It is expected that such full-color CDs will have a promising application prospect in the identification of jujube species.

Melamine and Citric acid were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co.,Ltd.(Shanghai, China). Other metal chloride of Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ag+, Sn2+, Co2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Fe2+ and Fe3+ were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co.,Ltd.(Shanghai, China). All deionized (DI) water throughout the work was purified through a Millipore system (Bedford, MA). Jujube samples were purchased from Wal-Mart.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken on JEOL TEM-2100. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were obtained on Thermo ESCALAB 250. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained on a Nicolet iS10 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo scientific, America). The UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on UV-2550 (Shimadzu, Japan) UV–vis spectrophotometer. The ultraviolet photoelectron spectra (UPS) were collected by Thermofisher EscaLab Xi+. The photoluminescent (PL) spectra were performed with a fluorescent spectrophotometer RF-5301PC (Shimadzu, Japan). The Raman spectra were recorded on DXR Raman microscope. The quantum yield and fluorescence lifetime were recorded using a steady-state & time-resolved fluorescence spectrofluorometer (Edinburgh FLS920). The jujube juices were obtained by juice extractor (Morphy richards MR9600).

The CDs are synthesized by adding citric acid and melamine in four different solvents (water, dimethylformamide, ethanol and formamide). 5 mmol of citric acid and 5 mmol of melamine were dissolved in 10 mL of different solvents, added to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and placed in an oven heated at 180 °C for 8 h. After the reaction was completed, the reaction solution was naturally cooled. Next, the precursor solution was centrifuged at 20,000 rpm for 8 min to dislodge precipitates and obtain a supernatant containing CDs. The resulting supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane filter to remove large particles. The CDs prepared by ethanol and formamide were further purified by silica column chromatography (eluent volume ratio: 95 % dichloromethane, 5% methanol). The resulting mixture of four CDs was dialyzed for 20 h, further frozen, and vacuum dried for 24 h for further use. It is then dispersed in water for characterization. A blue fluorescent sample obtained from aqueous solution is labeled as B-CDs. A green fluorescent sample obtained from dimethylformamide solvent was labeled as G-CDs. A yellow fluorescent sample obtained from ethanol is labeled as Y-CDs. A red fluorescent sample from formamide is labeled as R-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and quantitative analysis for Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of Fe3+ was carried out in an aqueous solution at room temperature. The fluorescence quenching efficiency of the probe was calculated according to the following formula: quenching efficiency = F/F0, Where F0 and F refer to the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the absence and presence of test metal ions. The selectivity of CDs was determined by comparison with other common metal cation solutions. In order to evaluate the sensitivity to Fe3+, different concentrations of Fe3+ were added and the mixed CDs solution was sonicated for 1 min, and then spectral measurement was performed. In addition, a similar procedure is performed to detect other metal ions (Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ag+, Sn2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe2+ and Fe3+). All chloride salts are used to avoid the effects of various anions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reaction of CDs with jujube juice extraction solution
ce:section.ce:para: The jujube juices were prepared by adding 10 g of different jujube into 50 mL deionized water, and then mashing in juice extractor for 2 min. The resulting mixture was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane filter and centrifuged at 20,000 rpm for 8 min to obtain a supernatant jujube juice. We collected 12 jujube samples (100 μL per sample) of each typical jujube (Hami Jujube, Grey Jujube, Premium Jujube, Xinjiang Jujube, Hotan Beach Jujube and Hotan Jujube), yielding a total of 72 samples. Each sample was placed in a 2 mL centrifuge tube and labeled with number in the range of J1–J72 (Hami Jujube: J1–J12, Grey Jujube: J13–J24, Premium Jujube: J25-J36, Xinjiang Jujube: J37-J48, Hotan Beach Jujube: J48-J60 and Hotan Jujube: J61-J72). Next, 1.9 mL of CDs solution was added to each sample for further measurement.

Our CDs were synthesized using citric acid and melamine as precursors in different solvents, including water, dimethylformamide, ethanol and formamide, respectively (Fig. 1 ). After the same experimental treatment, the CDs samples with different fluorescent emission colors of blue, green, yellow and red were obtained, labeled as B-CDs, G-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs.

The CDs were characterized by TEM, HRTEM, FT-IR, Raman and XPS. In Fig. 2 , the TEM images revealed that CDs were nearly spherical with average particle sizes of approximately 2.1, 3.4, 5.3 and 7.1 nm, respectively. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images clearly show that four typical CDs had similar observable lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) in-plane lattice [27,28].

The FT-IR spectra shown in Fig. 3 revealed that although these samples were from the same reactants, their absorptions were a bit different due to being treated in different solvents. However, these CDs all possessed OH/N–H (3393 cm−1), C − N (1048 cm−1) and CO (1722 cm−1) on their surfaces. These abundant hydrophilic groups guaranteed them an excellent solubility in aqueous solution [29,30]. Also, CC (1580 cm−1) was observed for four samples to indicate the formation of an aromatic structure in all CDs [31,32]. Besides, two other observations were paid attention to. One was that the absorption of N-containing groups was quite different in details. The absorption at 1387 and 1668 cm−1 attributing to typical tensile vibrations of CN = and CN bonds became more significant with red-shift of fluorescence emission, indicating that these CDs contained more graphitic nitrogen as the PL red-shift [33]. The other was that the stretching vibration of OH (3393 cm−1) are tiny in the pattern of Y-CDs. The solvent ethanol used for the synthesis of the Y-CDs affects the carboxylic acid of citric acid and forms amide bonds, leading to relatively poor sensitivity to metal ion consequently [34].

As shown in Figure S1, there were two peaks at 1344 and 1595 cm−1 in the Raman spectra of the four CDs, corresponding to defects (D band) and graphite (G band), respectively. And there was a significantly decreasing intensity of the G band and increasing intensity of the D band from B-CDs to R-CDs. However, the ID/IG ratios were very similar, which of four samples from B-CDs to R-CDs were 1.03, 1.02, 1.03 and 1.12, respectively. In our case, the greater number of defects in the R-CDs was due to the increased number of graphitic nitrogen atoms in the original sp2 domains, according to the following XPS analysis [8,35,36].

To investigate the composition of CDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were used to characterize the elemental information and chemical state of CDs. The XPS survey spectra in Figure S2 showed three typical peaks at 288.1, 399.1 and 532.1 eV for each sample, corresponding to C1 s, N1 s and O1 s. It meant that four samples were composed of the same elements. As listed in Table S1, the carbon and nitrogen content were approximately consistent. While the oxygen content in samples increased from 11.7 % to 18.4 % as PL red-shift, indicating a limited increase in oxidation. Fig. 4 illustrated the high-resolution XPS spectra of four CDs. The C1 s spectra can be fitted into three binding energy peaks, corresponding to O = CO (288.4 eV), CO/C-N (284.5 eV) and CC/C = C (283.7 eV) [37]. The O1 s spectra display two peaks at 531.2 and 529.4 eV, attributed to CO and CO bands, respectively [38]. The N1 s band contains two peaks at 400.3 and 398.6 eV, representing graphitic N, and amide N, respectively [39]. The high-resolution of C 1s spectra revealed that the relative contents of O=C-O of the samples at 288.4 eV were very approximate (Table S2). In contrast, graphitic nitrogen relative contents dramatically increased while amide nitrogen significantly decreased as the red-shift of PL emission, which was considered to be the most important parameter responsible for the PL red-shift transition.

In short, the data above mentioned clearly showed that the four CDs obtained using diverse solvents were rich in oxygen-containing/nitrogen-containing surface groups. It had a gradually larger conjugated sp2 domain, and their particle size, oxidation degree and graphitic nitrogen content increased simultaneously with the PL red-shift.

The UV − vis absorption spectra were investigated to study the optical properties of four typical CDs. As shown in Figure S3, four typical CDs display different absorption peaks in the lower-energy region. The absorption bands of four typical CDs were located in 330, 400, 470 and 570 nm, respectively, reflecting that these CDs possess diverse surface states [40–42].

Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) showed the secondary electron cutoff region on the left and the amplified spectrum near the Femi edge on the right (Figure S4). The energy of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) was calculated by the formula following. HOMO = - hν + (ECutoff – EFermi). The HOMO of the samples lie at -7.94, -8.17, -7.35 and -7.25 eV, respectively. According to the UV–vis spectra, the bandgaps of four CDs were 2.79, 2.27, 2.26 and 1.91 eV, respectively (estimated from the related Tauc plots shown in Figure S5). Then we calculated that the CDs lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) lie at -5.15, -5.9, -5.09 and -5.34 eV, respectively.

Fluorescence excitation−emission map and fluorescence spectra of four samples are shown in Fig. 5 . Although the emission wavelength of all samples moved more or less with the change of excitation wavelength, the variations in emission wavelength for B-CDs, Y-CDs and R-CDs were very narrow. Thus, B-CDs, Y-CDs and R-CDs could be considered they possess excitation-independent fluorescence properties, which emission wavelength were approximately at 450, 580, and 650 nm, respectively. In contrast, the G-CDs fluorescence was excitation-dependent. When the excitation wavelength increased, the emission wavelength of the G-CDs gradually changed in the range of 500 nm–600 nm. Generally, if the size distribution of CDs is sufficiently uniform, it has the property of excitation-independent. Thus, since the purification of CDs was not detailed enough, it was possible to appear different luminescence centers in G-CDs and excitation-dependent behavior. In addition, the high reproducibility of the synthesis different CDs has been clearly demonstrated. As shown in Figure S4, under the same synthesis process, the fluorescence spectra of the three parallel batches of CDs were roughly the same. Moreover, CDs showed considerable stability after being stored in environmental conditions for 3 months, with no significant attenuation in fluorescence intensity (Figure S5).

The time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectra (Figure S6 and Table S3) indicated that they could be fitted by dual-exponential Formula. The time-resolved PL decay curve involving a short-lived component τ1 (about 1 ns) which were assigned to the recombination processes in the core states, and a long-lived component τ2 (about 10 ns) which were assigned to the recombination processes in the surface states, respectively [43,44]. It was distinct that as a similar average lifetime of approximately 6 ns, the τ1 proportion increased from 34.18% to 97.20%, implying that the role of core states in the fluorescence lifetime of these samples enhanced with their PL red-shift [9,45]. The quantum yields (QY) for four selected CDs dissolved in aqueous solution were 10.95 %, 9.56 %, 5.53 % and 4.28 %, respectively. It should be mentioned that the QY of R-CDs was 4.28 %, which was the result that the luminescent center was destroyed due to extreme carbonization [46].

The formation mechanism of CDs was explored, as shown in Fig. 6 . In the initial reaction stage, the cross-linked molecules formed during the dehydration process were amorphous. They were further condensed into a carbon core with parts of branched functional groups until the carbonized nanoparticles were finally formed. The formation approach displayed that the precursors were necessary to undergo a dehydration reaction to produce polymer dots first and then further be carbonized into CDs [47]. Therefore, the particle size and degree of graphitization highly depended on the dehydration and carbonization conditions [48]. In our case, the four solvents with different instinct properties affected the formation of the CDs process. From water to ethanol, the boiling points of the solvents descend, leading to the increased dehydration and carbonization of the reactants at a vapor pressure of 180 °C. Compared with dimethylformamide, formamide has higher nitrogen reactivity, further leading to increased graphite nitrogen content in samples. Thus, different solvents resulted in diverse physical and chemical conditions for dehydration and carbonization processes, which substantially affected the optical properties of CDs samples.

A reasonable explanation for the red-shift of luminescence is the size dependence of the electronic structure of CDs [49,50]. It has been shown that the luminescence of CDs came from the radiation recombination of both photoexcited electrons in the surface trap state and the generated holes in the HOMO level [51–53]. The research has found that the energy level of the surface trap state was independent of size [54], so it can be concluded that the HOMO level could vary with size. Larger particle sizes resulted in a higher density of states to increase the HOMO level (Fig. 6) [49]. Thus, particle size can affect the bandgap, resulting in a red-shift in fluorescence. Another factor that caused the red-shift of photoluminescence was the increase in the amount of graphitic nitrogen in the CDs, which was confirmed by the characterization of XPS and FT-IR. The graphite nitrogen created a mid-gap state in the HOMO-LUMO gap to narrow the bandgap, which was clearly demonstrated by the characterization of UV–vis and UPS, resulting in significant red-shift emission. This was due to the excess electrons provided to the unoccupied π-orbitals of the conjugate system [55]. These findings provided a reasonable hypothesis for the mechanism by which the increase in graphite nitrogen content in the CDs caused red-shift fluorescence.

CDs have been widely used to detect various metal ions by fluorescence quenching or enhancement. We studied the fluorescence emission intensity in the presence of different metal ions showing that the presence of Fe3+ in four CDs aqueous solutions produced a significant quenching effect compared to other metal ions at the same concentration (Fig. 7 A). The unique fluorescence quenching effect of CDs indicated their good selectivity to Fe3+. The results showed that these four CDs had the best selectivity for iron ion and can clearly distinguish between Fe2+ and Fe3+.

In order to discuss the lower detection limit of CDs for Fe3+, we needed to further optimize the test conditions. As we know, Fe3+ will be hydrolyzed in alkaline solution environment, so it is meaningful to test the detection performance of CDs in different pH buffers. First, we compared the fluorescence spectra of CDs solutions at different pH values. As shown in Figure S9, the fluorescence intensity of CDs has little change in the pH range of 3–10, indicating that the pH range has little effect on the fluorescence intensity of CDs. Next we discussed the detection performance of CDs on Fe3+ in different pH buffers. As shown in Figure S10, the fluorescence intensity of CDs did not change in the acidic, neutral and alkaline solution environment of Fe3+, indicating that the sensing performance of CDs to Fe3+ did not affect by the pH of buffer. Therefore, we monitored the fluorescence intensity of CDs in a solution with a pH of 7. The different concentrations of Fe3+ ions were dropped into four CDs to study their quenching characteristics and establish calibration curves (Figs. 7B-E). Error bars represent standard deviations for three repeated experiments. The figures visually showed that the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased as the Fe3+ increased. Therefore, these four CDs can be used as nanoprobes for fluorescent Fe3+ detection. As expected, the quenching efficiency (F0/F) has a good linear relationship with the Fe3+ concentration.
B-CDs: F0/F = 1.061 + 0.004C(μmol/L)(R2 = 0.986, C : 2.5–250 μM)
G-CDs: F0/F = 1.017 + 0.014C(μmol/L)(R2 = 0.985, C : 0.5–50 μM)
Y-CDs: F0/F = 1.021 + 0.001C(μmol/L)(R2 = 0.916, C : 25–2500 μM)
R-CDs: F0/F = 0.994 + 0.004C(μmol/L)(R2 = 0.993, C : 2.5–500 μM)
Here, F0 and F correspond to the fluorescence intensity in the presence and absence of metal ions, and C represents the concentration of Fe3+. Further, the limit of detection (LOD) of B-CDs, G-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs was calculated as 500 nM, 100 nM, 10 μM and 1 μM, respectively. The results showed that the four samples had different sensitivity and detection range, which provided diverse choices for various application themes to detect trace Fe3+ feasibly. Compared with previously published optical/fluorescence-based methods on the detection of Fe3+, the as prepared CDs have lower limit of detection and wider linear range. Table 1 gives a brief comparison of optical/fluorescence based methods on the detection of Fe3+.

According to previous reports, selective photoluminescence quenching is believed to be related to the rapid electron transfer process between Fe3+ and CDs. In order to further confirm the fluorescence quenching mechanism, a time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) experiment was used to test the fluorescence lifetime of G-CDs in the presence and absence of Fe3+. As shown in Figure S11, the average fluorescence lifetime of the absence and presence of Fe3+ are 6 and 3 ns, respectively, which directly illustrated the decay behavior of fluorescence in the presence of Fe3+. Therefore, according to the fluorescence lifetime, it is strongly confirmed that there is a dynamic fluorescence quenching effect between CDs and Fe3+ through the electron transfer process [57]. Fe3+ ions have a higher affinity to functional groups containing N and O on the surface of CDs [63,64]. Fe3+ can absorb the CDs surface and coordinate with a large amount of −OH on the CDs edge. As a result of this coordination or chelation cooperation, the electron portion of the C excited state is transferred to the unfilled d orbital of Fe3+. Thus, electron transition (fluorescence emission) in the form of radiation is inhibited, leading to non-radiative electron/hole recombination, which further leads to significant fluorescence quenching [65]. Among all samples, Y-CDs exhibited the worst detection performance, which could be attributed to the surface not being rich in hydroxyl functional groups.

The identification of jujube species by spectroscopic techniques combined with stoichiometry is a promising research field [66]. The quality of different species of jujube is different, and so is the content of Fe3+. Therefore, different species of jujube can quench the fluorescence of CDs with varying amplitudes. Besides that, different species of jujube cause slight changes in intensity at certain wavelengths in the fluorescence spectra of CDs solution. Since human eyes cannot determine the characteristic information of the spectral data, we can use the multivariate resolution and calibration methods in chemometrics instead. Principal component analysis (PCA) is a statistical method for processing multivariate data, fully compressing the original data and extracting highly representative characteristic variables from the original data [67–69]. Bayesian discriminant analysis method was used to establish the discriminant model of jujube. Bayesian discriminant analysis can obtain posterior probability from prior probability based on new information [70–72]. SPSS Statistics software was used for statistical analysis in this study.

Six different species of jujube were chosen, including Hami Jujube, Grey Jujube, Premium Jujube, Xinjiang Jujube, Hotan Beach Jujube and Hotan Jujube. The 12 jujube extraction samples of each typical jujube were collected, yielding a total of 72 samples. Fluorescence spectra were obtained by adding different jujube juices samples (J1-J72) into CDs solution (Fig. 8 A). We used SPSS software to perform PCA and Bayes discriminant analysis on the fluorescence spectra of the reaction between CDs and jujube samples. The results are listed in Table S4. PCi represents the i-th principal component. The proportion of PCi variance to the total variance is called the contribution rate. A higher value of the contribution rate indicated a stronger ability to synthesis the information of the PCi. We found that the contribution rate was the highest when there were three principal components, that is, the use of three-dimensional feature can more comprehensively synthesize the information of the spectral data. The cumulative contribution rate of three PCi (PC1, PC2 and PC3) for B-CDS, G-CDS, Y-CDS and R-CDS is 88.135 %, 95.324 %, 97.554 % and 98.596 %, respectively. The classification accuracy and cross-validation accuracy of all samples are satisfactory and can be used for subsequent data analysis to meet the requirements of establishing a discriminant model. The highest value of R-CDS was 98.596 %. The spatial distribution of the principal component analysis is shown in Fig. 8B, and various jujubes can be clearly identified. Based on the above spectral information of R-CDs, Bayesian discriminant analysis was used to generate the discriminant function as follows, where X1 and X2 represent fluorescence intensity values at 390 and 531 nm, respectively.
Hami Jujube: Y= -17.545−22.116X1+37.976X2
Grey Jujube: Y= -263.71 + 357.945X1-73.808X2
Premium Jujube: Y= -43.84 + 120.569X1-4.543X2
Xinjiang Jujube: Y= -60.513−157.877X1+19.466X2
Hotan Beach Jujube: Y= -16.648−79.262X1+9.633X2
Hotan Jujube: Y= -136−219.26X1+11.267X2

When the spectral information of an unknown jujube are inserted into the discriminant function, the species of the jujube could be indicated by which function gets the most considerable value. The fluorescence spectrum of the reaction of CDs with jujube samples and the spatial distribution of its principal component analysis for B-CDs, G-CDs and Y-CDs are shown in Figure S12−14. Furthermore, the discriminant function of B-CDs, G-CDs and Y-CDs are given in the Supporting Information. All four CDs were able to distinguish jujube species clearly, and among them, R-CDs had the highest resolution.

In summary, we employed citric acid and melamine as reactants to obtain full-color fluorescent CDs covering the entire visible region by adjusting the type of reaction solvent. Detailed characterization analysis indicated that the solvent controlled the dehydration carbonization process, resulting in the increase of quantum size, oxidation degree and graphitic nitrogen content, which were the core factors leading to the red-shift of CDs. And a new formation mechanism, including dehydration and carbonization processes, was proposed. The as-prepared CDs can be used as fluorescent sensors for Fe3+ ion detection. The food-tasting sensor of jujube species was established successfully by using PCA and Bayes discriminant analysis. The CDs fluorescent sensor has good detection ability and classification accuracy for different species of jujube without additional processing of spectral data.

Guannan Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft, Validation. Dehao Kong: Validation, Investigation, Visualization. Jiayin Han: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Ri Zhou: Validation, Investigation. Yuan Gao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Zepei Wu: Writing - review & editing. Lianjing Zhao: Methodology, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition. Chenguang Wang: Investigation. Lijun Wang: Visualization. Geyu Lu: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of Interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Jilin Province (20200201289JC), Science and Technology Project of Jilin Provincial Department of Education (JJKH20200982KJ), and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2019T120238, 2018M640282).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2021.129963.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical tuning of luminescent carbon nanodots: from preparation to luminescence mechanism

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Bao, Z.L. Zhang, Z.Q. Tian, L. Zhang, C. Liu, Y. Lin, et al., Electrochemical Tuning of Luminescent Carbon Nanodots: From Preparation to Luminescence Mechanism, Adv Mater, 23 (2011) 5801-5806.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photoluminescence-tunable carbon nanodots: surface-state energy-gap tuning

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Bao, C. Liu, Z.L. Zhang, D.W. Pang, Photoluminescence-Tunable Carbon Nanodots: Surface-State Energy-Gap Tuning, Adv Mater, 27 (2015) 1663-1667.
S.N. Baker, G.A. Baker, Luminescent Carbon Nanodots: Emergent Nanolights, Angew Chem-Int Edit, 49 (2010) 6726-6744.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.Y. Xu, R. Ray, Y.L. Gu, H.J. Ploehn, L. Gearheart, K. Raker, et al., Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments, J Am Chem Soc, 126 (2004) 12736-12737.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Bilge, H.M. Velioglu, B. Sezer, K.E. Eseller, I.H. Boyaci, Identification of meat species by using laser-induced breakdown spectroscopy, Meat Science, 119 (2016) 118-122.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantitative analysis of adulteration of extra virgin olive oil using Raman spectroscopy improved by Bayesian framework least squares support vector machines

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Dong, Y. Zhang, B. Zhang, X. Wang, Quantitative analysis of adulteration of extra virgin olive oil using Raman spectroscopy improved by Bayesian framework least squares support vector machines, Analytical Methods, 4 (2012) 2772-2777.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel fusion approach based on induced ordered weighted averaging operators for chemometric data analysis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. AlHichri, Y. Bazi, N. Alajlan, F. Melgani, S. Malek, R.R. Yager, A novel fusion approach based on induced ordered weighted averaging operators for chemometric data analysis, Journal of Chemometrics, 27 (2013) 447-456.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bayesian variable selection for Gaussian process regression: application to chemometric calibration of spectrometers

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Chen, B. Wang, Bayesian variable selection for Gaussian process regression: Application to chemometric calibration of spectrometers, Neurocomputing, 73 (2010) 2718-2726.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Guannan Liu received his BE degree from Jilin University of China in 2015. He is currently working toward the Ph.D. degree in the Electronics Science and Engineering department, Jilin University. His current researches focus on the synthesis of carbon dots and their applications.

Dehao Kong received his BE degree from Jilin University of China in 2019.Now he is working for a MS degree in the Electronics Science and Engineering department, Jilin University. His current researches focus on the preparation and application of mesoporous semiconductor oxide, especially in gas sensor.

Jiayin Han received her BE degree from Jilin University of China in 2020. Now she is working for a MS degree in the Electronics Science and Engineering department, Jilin University. Her current researches focus on the synthesis and application of graphene and semiconductor oxide, especially in gas sensor.

Ri Zhou received his BE degree from Jilin University of China in 2015. He is currently working toward the Ph.D. degree in the Electronics Science and Engineering department, Jilin University. His current researches focus on the synthesis of small molecule fluorescent probe and their applications.

Yuan Gao received her PhD degree from Department of Analytical Chemistry at Jilin University in 2012. Now she is an associate professor in Jilin University, China. Her current research is focus on the preparation and application of graphene and semiconductor oxide, especial in gas sensor and biosensor.

Zepei Wu received his BE degree from Jilin University of China in 2017.Now he is studying for a MS degree in the Electronics Science and Engineering department, Jilin University. He is interested in the synthesis and application of graphene and semiconductor oxide, especially in gas sensor.

Lianjing Zhao received her MS degree from Jilin University of China in 2013. She is currently working toward the Ph.D. degree in the Electronics Science and Engineering department, Jilin University. Her research interests mainly focus on the on the development of the functional nanomaterials for biosensors.

Chenguang Wang received his PhD degree from the College of Chemistry, Jilin University in 2013. He then joined the Institute of Transformative Bio-Molecules, Nagoya University as a postdoctoral fellow. In 2019, he joined the College of Electronic Science and Engineering, Jilin University as a professor. His research interests focus on the design and synthesis of organic fluorescent molecules and their applications in fluorescence bio-imaging.

Lijun Wang received the B. Sci. degree in electronic sciences in 1973 from Jilin University in China. Now he is a professor of Changchun Institute of Optics, Fine Mechanics and Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences. His current research interests in basic and applied research of laser technology and other fields.

Geyu Lu received the B. Sci. degree in electronic sciences in 1985 and the M. Sci. degree in 1988 from Jilin University in China and the Dr. Eng. degree in 1998 from Kyushu University in Japan. Now he is a professor of Jilin University, China. His current research interests include the development of chemical sensors and the application of the function materials.
